steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence opens with a participial phrase: “Having devised an optical spectroscopy technique...” A participial phrase at the start of a sentence must modify the grammatical subject of the main clause — the subject must be the person who did the devising. That person is Ahmed Zewail.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘chemist Ahmed Zewail introduced a new scientific discipline’: Ahmed Zewail is the subject, so the opening phrase correctly attaches to him — he devised the technique and introduced the discipline. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘a new scientific discipline was introduced by Ahmed Zewail, a chemist’: The subject is “a new scientific discipline” — the discipline did not devise the technique. Dangling modifier. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘the introduction of a new scientific discipline was initiated by chemist Ahmed Zewail’: The subject is “the introduction” — an abstract noun that cannot have devised anything. Dangling modifier. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘chemist Ahmed Zewail's introduction of a new scientific discipline would be’: The subject is “chemist Ahmed Zewail’s introduction” (a noun phrase, not Zewail himself), and “would be” introduces a hypothetical that contradicts the established past-tense context. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
